speaker: interviewer
text: April 24, 1996. Survivor Sonja Waitzner, born Friedman, and this is tape number two. So we were just talking about the trip away from Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We arrived in Aachen. My father had arranged for a guide to lead us into Belgium. It was at the three-corner [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] countries; Belgium, Luxembourg, and Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He paid-- he paid the guide a heap of money. And it was arranged that night he would take us over the border. I remember going through woods. And suddenly, the guide disappeared, and the Gestapo is there. He was a double agent. He took our money and disappeared. We were caught, brought back to Aachen. My father and brothers were put into prison, and I was put into prison. In a common prison, at the age of 11 and 1/2, I spent 24 hours. My father and my two brothers together. My brothers spent 24 hours. And we were-- the children were released after 24 hours. My father spent seven days. When I was up in the cell with two women, and I saw my father and brothers walking in the courtyard of-- prison courtyard, walking with the other prisoners, I started crying and screaming, Papa, Papa, Papa! And he looked up, and I thought his heart would break. And now my heart was broken. They brought me food, and I refused to eat it. I was called down to the guard. He had pity on me, and he offered me some food of his own, and I refused. And I went back to the window, and I cried, Papa, Papa, Papa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the beginning of a nightmare that I cannot forget. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 24 hours later, the three of us are released. We are brought to a Jewish family that took us in. And for seven days, we waited until my father was released from prison. He was told, and he had to sign papers, that he can never return back to Aachen. The three of us had to leave back to Vienna. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to the orphanage, and my brothers and my father, I don't remember where they stayed. But maybe with my uncle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because my uncle, I know, had affidavit to go to Palestine with his family. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] A month later, Leon said to Papa, Papa, Norbert and I are going over the border by ourselves. I'm not staying here. But then, they were 14 and 16. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At-- but at the time, my uncle also went to Germany. And I don't remember. I think he also tried to go illegally to Belgium first. And he said he's going to go with them. They are going to go with them, so they're not alone, but they are going over the border by themselves. Sure enough, Leon and Norbert took a guide and crossed the border by themselves into Belgium. Two kids. My father could not leave. He had to see what's going to be with me. My brothers then put actions in motion with the Belgian government to get me out legally. And it finally was agreed by the German government and Belgian government that the childrens' transport is going to go out from Austria to Belgium, and I was among the children. When that was assured, my father, again, went illegally over the border. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, by the way, before I forget. When we went to first illegally to the border, and we were caught, my father had to throw away his money that he had. Because if they would have caught him with money, they would have put him into a concentration camp then, because of smuggling of money into a foreign country. But I forgot something else. I have to go back again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in Vienna Kristallnacht [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we first returned. And we were at my uncle's house, the three of us, the three children, and my father was not there. And we were scared. They were breaking in the-- in the synagogues, and they were going on a rampage that was unheard of. And my father wasn't there. And my father got caught. And they had him sweep the glass from the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they wanted to take him in a concentration camp. And he said, I served in the Austrian army. You're going to take me away? I served in your army. So they let him go, and he came back to us very late at night, grey in the face. And that's when Leon said, Papa, I'm getting out of here. Now I'm at the point where my father left, and I came to Belgium with the childrens' transport. Do you remember that transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A train full of children, all to Belgium, Jewish children, that had relatives over there in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you take some belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Belongings? I had no belongings. I had a few clothes. I had nothing. I mean, we've already, you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was very little left. You can say I had the clothes on my back and maybe a small valise. Maybe I had a small valise, that's it, from the childrens' home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with any people that you knew on that--

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember anybody. I don't remember. All I remember, I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father give you instructions before he left? Did you somehow know what was expected?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that I was going to be safe. I knew I was going-- the date was set for me to leave legally. And I was glad that I was going to go by train, because that ordeal through the forest, and-- and the prison was something that I really didn't want to go through anymore. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Blanca, in the meantime, had gone to England. And I went to live with a family, a Christian family. As a matter of fact, the woman was a German woman, and the husband was a Belgian man. And they took me in. And that was at the Dutch-- near the Dutch border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hollar and Schryvers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. S-C-H-R-Y-V-E-R-S. That's a Flemish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what town or village? Do you know the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Limburg. Limburg, near the Dutch border. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were very good to me. As a matter of fact, they wanted to adopt me. But there was no question about it. This was just borrowed time. I'm going finally home. In the meantime, Blanca came from England to Belgium. And that's when I went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Was that in winter, then, or was that later? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, excuse me. My childrens' transport left in March of 1939. That's when I left. My father left late in 1938, end of-- or maybe beginning of 1939, very shortly before me, until everything was arranged for me. Thank God he made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came in March.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of 1939, to Belgium, legally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a note? Did you hear from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my-- my father came to visit me. Oh, yeah. And we were in contact constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brothers, sure. We're very close. When-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when Blanca came from London-- my stepmother-- I was finally able to go back to Brussels, and we were one family together. Actually, that was the first time I lived together with my family since I was eight years old. And by then I was 11 and a half. No, I was already 12. I'm sorry. I was already 12. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went to school. First, I went to school in Limburg, and I learned Flemish. Was very easy. Was fast. Then I went to Brussels, and I went to school, and I learned French. But I didn't know the language at all. I-- I almost dozed off. The only thing I understood was math and sewing, and drawing. That's-- that's all I understood. I was almost falling asleep in class. And gradually, gradually, I learned French.

speaker: interviewer
text: So really, if I say my formal education ended at the age of 11, it is true, because I had to learn foreign languages. And by the time I learned, the war broke out, and that was the end of everything. How did your living situation look like in Brussels? Where did your family stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brussels was-- was very cramped. We had a-- a huge room and a kitchen, I believe. And that room  was huge, and there were beds and beds. What can I tell you? That was the immigration. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do? Could your father work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, by then, was sick. He suffered from arteriosclerosis. But he did some work, whatever could be found, but not too hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly it was-- pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Go Ahead

speaker: interviewee
text: We were also assisted by the Jewish Committee, because we were really without means, since my father gave practically everything to the guide and had to throw away his money. We were really very hard up for money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers were still in school. We were all in school. And, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had an uncle in Antwerp that was my father's brother. And he lived, originally, in Vienna, too. But he didn't get along too well with that uncle, Max. They were in business together, the three of-- the three brothers. And it-- something must have happened in business. He left and he went to Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oscar. And I adored him. A handsome man. Ay, he was so handsome. I adored that man. He got married in Antwerp. We all went to Antwerp. It was a wonderful experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the war broke out. It was a short time thereafter. It was-- I came in March '39, and a year later, that was it. Half a year was in Limburg, and then a very short period. By then, I spoke French already pretty well. The war broke out, and it was havoc. My father was recognized by the Germans as an alien. And he was sent, as an alien, like he would be German. Like he would be German. He was sent to Saint-Cyprien, in the south of France, interned there. German Jews and Austrian Jews were considered aliens in Belgium. They made no difference between Jews and non-Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a place called Gurs, my father was gone suddenly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shortly after the war started. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was terrible. Within 10 days, Belgium was occupied. This-- the war started May 10, and a week later, it was-- 10 days later it was occupied. France fell a few weeks later. Big, mighty France. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was bad. Then we got a notification from the Brussels that my brother and I-- Oh. Excuse me. My father eventually escaped there from Saint Cyprien He had forged papers, and he had a lot of guts, and he looked nothing-- not at all Jewish, was a handsome man. And when the Germans went through the train to look at the papers of the people, my father opened his wallet and took some paper, and when he came, he just put it in, as if he'd already shown. And he bluffed his way through, and he went back to Belgium. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was really a wonderful reunion. He told the stories that when he had a piece of bread, they hung it from the ceiling because the rats were going everywhere. And the rats were going on the string to get to the piece of bread, too. It was horrible. Horrible. That was in Saint Cyprien in Gurs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were really persecuted, not only as Jews, but as corroborators with the Germans. That was the irony. When my father came back, we got the notification that all Jews-- first of all, we all had to wear stars. The yellow stars. We got notification that we have to register as Jews. And we should get valises together, and clothes, and we are all going to go to work camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, my brother was already-- Leon was already bigger, and my father talked things over with Leon. And Leon said, Papa, no. We are not going. We are going into hiding. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We are going to split up. Norbert and I, we are going separate. You, Blanca, and Sonja, you go separate. We are going into hiding, and that's it. No more stars. No stars. And that was it. My brothers went, found an apartment illegally, and we went and found an apartment illegally.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brussels. And that's where my father, Blanca, and I lived until I was caught. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Were very, very hard times. I-- the moment the war started, everybody ended with the school. Jews couldn't go to school, anyway. And we all had to go to work to earn money. I was 12 years old. I went to work for three young children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to help feed them, clean the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the afternoon, walk the children. And I was a child myself. And then the starvation started. No matter how much we all worked, we made very, very little money. We were the-- in hiding. We had to get false papers. When I was on the outside, I had no problems. I was fluent in French. As a girl, it was fine. I could go anywhere. I was safe. Except if I went home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or I was in somebody else's home that was Jewish, it was a problem. That job with the children I could not take for long. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I went to work for a woman. She was from Germany. She was not Jewish, but her husband was Jewish. And she was-- she made brassieres. And I worked for her, making brassieres. She and I worked together. That was it. I remember she had a-- an attic upstairs. And we used to practice hiding, in case the Nazis came. And I remember, when we climbed up to hide, I almost became hysterical. I started to laugh like crazy. This was nervous laughter. Was nothing to laugh about. I got-- when I got scared, I started to laugh. And I said, if this ever happens to me, I have to hide, I'm going to be giving myself away. I cannot stop laughing. That was hysteria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very hard work. This woman had an electric machine, but I didn't. So I was-- eight hours a day, I was going with my feet, sewing. And I was highly undernourished. Our rations were so small. You see, I had to keep my regular papers to get my rations, otherwise, I couldn't get rations. And the false papers, they stayed on me for identification, to keep me safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your employer, did this woman know that you were Jewish, or did [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, her husband was Jewish. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could tell her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Of course. She immigrated with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was getting thinner and thinner. And now I went for lunch. And we ate rutabaga. Do you ever hear of a vegetable called rutabaga? It's a big vegetable. It's orange in kind. Here it is a specialty. When I see the word rutabaga, I-- I-- I remember the horror of it, because it's a big vegetable. It's orange inside. It has a peel on the outside. And I used to go on-- on Saturday, when I didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, I didn't work Saturday? I worked six days. On Sunday, I used to go to my brothers' to cook and to clean. And I used to cook for them. But for that, I got another free meal. So I used to take the rutabaga, cut them in-- in cubes, like potatoes, grate them, cut them in slices. And no matter how you sliced it, and no matter how you cooked it, it was rutabaga. And I ate quantities of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And no matter how much I ate, I got thinner and thinner. Norbert was sick. He had already tuberculosis of the bones. Completely undernourished. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very handsome fellow. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do I bring this out? I know a lot-- a lot of people during the war, the occupied countries, they were all, that didn't have money, they were all going hungry, period. But I was growing up, and something awaited me for which I needed a lot of strength. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So this went on for a few years. I worked for that woman about, I would say, two years. I was really not a well girl. I had blue marks on my legs. I had a lot of rashes in my face. I suffered from malnutrition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I worked for-- I worked for somebody. She was a dressmaker. And she sent me once to a home of Belgian people to shorten a skirt for a young person my age. And I came to that home, and I couldn't believe that people lived like this. It was gorgeous. It was a home like this, and even more beautiful. Much more beautiful. And the lady of the house looked at me. And she saw that I'm a kid, myself. She gave me, I remember, a whole box full of rice. This was so great. I didn't even know how to shorten, really, a skirt. Well, I did the best I could all the time, anyway. Good or bad, I did my best. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The only thing I really liked, the only work I ever liked, was my last job. I went to work for Italians in a factory that made pottery, and artists were painting on plates.

speaker: interviewer
text: I sat next to an elderly man with a beard, and I loved that man. He-- he painted on plates, landscapes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was then about 17 years old. The owners were Italians. They were personal friends of-- what's the dictator of Italy? Mussolini?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were friends of Mussolini, but they hated Hitler. They knew I was Jewish. They had a few Jewish people working there. And the Germans, the Nazis came to inquire. The Nazis, wherever they came, I was completely safe. It didn't matter. I was safe. I had false papers. They didn't ask my papers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the only job I really liked, but I had to walk a lot. I had to walk a lot, and I was really starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you paint, also, on ceramics? Or what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just made-- I just made certain little flowers. But I really loved to watch that man. He was a fine, great artist. And those-- and the-- the owners were called Rigoli. They were so nice. What a nice Italian couple. Nice people, anyway, in general, the Italians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have, at that time, any contact with youngsters your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I want to tell you something. I had no contact with anybody, except my brothers. I knew my-- Leon's wife then, Gerda, whom you're going to meet later. I knew her parents. That's another story. I don't-- She has a terrible tragedy, herself. But I knew her, and occasionally, her mother used to come to our house with Gerda, and she always had an eye on my brother, Leon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought she came for me, but she really came for Leon. [LAUGHS] They later married.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We have to stop this tape here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
